reviews.review_text:
Wow, after waiting for so many years, finally playing the complete Wintermute story mode feels surreal and deeply nostalgic—especially after seeing my last save state from 2019, back when I was still in high school.

But why did I even wait so long, when there are plenty of other survival games on Steam? Well, it's because for me, The Long Dark is the ultimate survival game of them all, while others in the genre eventually lean more into sandbox, creative gameplay. Here, everything wants to kill you all the time, whether it's the freezing cold or the wildlife. Only in this game do I truly feel that being alive isn't something that just exists—it is something you actively need to protect, especially during hard times. The moment you leave your hideout, not knowing if you will return alive, captures the true essence of survival.

Gameplay-wise, you also feel that everything revolves around staying alive. You quickly learn to focus on conserving as little energy as possible. You can get fatigued very quickly when constantly sprinting, which drastically reduces your crucial inventory capacity. That’s why The Long Dark is one of the few games that forces you into a more natural pace of movement. Instead of the classic running-and-stamina-recovery cycle, you mostly walk, especially when there is a long way to go. Still, I must admit it was a little boring to just leave your visible stamina completely unused. Maybe the developers should have introduced a fast-walking or jogging option.

When it comes to the difficulty, I am a bit torn. On the medium difficulty—which is how the game is intended to be played—you experience the aforementioned fast fatigue on one hand, but a way too generous resource distribution in the open areas on the other. Over time, this results in you skipping countless items because your inventory is already full. The cold might still be dangerous, but because of the abundance of supplies, I never faced any real issues with starvation or dehydration during my entire playtime. While it didn't completely ruin the survival aspect, it still feels like a missed opportunity to make the world more unforgiving. All in all, though, the difficulty is well-balanced—neither frustratingly hard nor too easy, as long as you don't abuse the checkpoint saves too much.

Presenting the game in a stylized, cartoony art style was a fantastic choice. It completely sets it apart from other titles in the genre and makes the graphics of The Long Dark virtually timeless. They could publish this game today, and no one would guess that it has already been on a 13-year journey. The only things that feel slightly dated are the simplified controls—almost like out of an old Slender Man mod—and the loading screens when entering buildings, though these are incredibly minor issues.


But now comes the biggest question: Was the long wait for the story mode, Wintermute, actually worth it?

Without getting into any spoilers, chapters one, two, and even three were great, perfectly supporting the unique gameplay loop. However, while chapters four and five retain some of those elements, they focus way too heavily on extra gimmicks and scripted scenarios just to force a sense of spectacle. Chapter five is especially guilty of this, steering the experience too far away from the original core survival gameplay. It is truly unfortunate that this happened to the very chapter we waited the longest for.

Consequently, the story—which started out so promising and left me desperate to see how it would end—lost a lot of its quality in the finale. In my opinion, it wasn't the epic conclusion I wanted after spending so many hours with the protagonists. Instead of ending everything with a bang, it constantly throws new plot threads and lore at you, almost as if it's denying being the final chapter and acting like we are only halfway through.

My Conclusion:
In the end, I am fine with the mediocre ending, but above all, I am incredibly happy that The Long Dark actually received a proper conclusion after all these years. It was a true pleasure to finally finish a journey I started 7 years ago. This game will hold a special place in my gaming heart forever. For those who have never played it, I still highly recommend it. The Long Dark is great, and it remains the truest survival experience in the entire genre. It is completely worth your time.

Just finished the Wintermute story mode out of pure stubbornness. It took the devs almost decade to release these 5 episodes. And at the end of it all... the result isn't just bad - it's one of the worst written pieces of media I had the misfortune of experiencing. The whole thing feels like the first draft that never got edited or revised. It should be studied how can a team of creatives have all this time and resources, and end up with such a boring, trite, painful story.

This is gonna be my first long review for any game. I just couldn't keep quiet after finishing Episode 5 of Wintermute.
I probably have around 500 hours spent in the game, starting from first episodes of story mode in 2018, then switching to survival or even trying to speedrun some of challenges. I was enjoying every mode of the game and only problems were very long breaks between story episodes, but it was still fine, especially regarding Covid-19 situation. So before Episode 5 it was great experience. Waiting for last episode was toooo loooong, 4,5 years is enough to make a new game, not just an episode. Overall it took 9!!! years to release last 3 episodes. And I would understand if last episode would be the greatest one but here comes big BUT - 
[b] It's ♥♥♥♥, old decayed ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that even dogs and dung beetles are not interested in [/b]
Level of disappointment is comparable to effect from last season of Lost TV series.
Now in details. Things that I liked - sounds, voices, Mathis as character and short time of exploring the ship. For everyone involved in these - thank you, maybe because of you I didn't drop game.
Bad things will go in order of appearing during gameplay and will contain spoilers.
[spoiler] Why do characters constantly lose clothes and gear? Between Ep2 and Ep4 it was the only understandable case. Why Astrid lost everything, is it a new town tradition to rob and undress visitors? How did Mackenzie lose his clothing after Ep4 and most insane DURING EP 5! They even undress him in ending cutscene!!! And what was the point to teach how craft stuff in first episodes and gear characters if everything is taken away anyway.
Perseverance Mills doesn't feel like real town, it looks like only people living there are bartender and dj. Others are strangers or lying dead somewhere else.
Next locations are empty and boring (except ship, there is good scary atmosphere). There are no sidequests anymore, no world lore notes, almost no notes overall. Only main story left.
And it's awful. No logic in many moments, doctor Larsen gone god knows where and why, water in lungs no idea why, guys on ship are frozen while there a lot of wooden crates around, captain died from what, Scene with Mackenzie, Jace and convicts it's a shame. Seriously. 4,5 years for that is a crime. 
Mackenzie is doing everything other people want from him without proper or sometimes any explanation while he has his own goal and being on timer. No one overall explains ♥♥♥♥ (WHAT A MYSTERY) and then you will be fed with ALL explanation in a row in 10-min cutscene. His mission to turn on powerplants or whatever it was is very boring. 3 wolves, turn on, telephone, repeat. Cougar is dead. Long walk with Jace is terrible, one phrase once a minute from her, great dialogue and pacing. Atwood saying machine working will take hours and then decrypts data in a moment. From 40 to 400 years and they are planning to sit in a bunker. And what after, they will go nuts like others after supplies will end and people will have to go out. Train section was fine. Where and how Jeremiah and Co did come from. Why convicts keep spawning. Why are they blind, deaf and stupid, but see you with their back. And so on. Long boring Dark. [/spoiler]

If you want to play survival, do challenges - it's a great game. If you want story and don't mind being dissappointed - play first 4 episodes. Last one read from someone's review. You will be dissappointed anyway, but it will save you few hours not doing boring things while going somewhere in a straight line.

As requested by the credits, I'm here to leave a review. Do yourself a favor and uninstall after episode 4. There is nothing of value beyond that, whatever you were wanting out of the final episode, it is not there. Everything about episode 5 is garabge, the acting, the scripting, the dialogue, the "gameplay" (lack of), no continuity, no reasoning, no logic, it is truly devoid of anything that makes a game enjoyable, unless you like key mashing quick time events that have zero purpose. It is a disgrace to what was one of my top 3 games of all time, dissolved into a pile of vomit. There is no way they can be proud of what this is, especially taking into account wasting 5 years for what amounted to a walking simulator on rails.

Beautiful sandbox, Beautiful art style, always a reason to come back to the game over the past 10 years. BUT Episode 5's ending was ridiculous and to wait for as long as we have for what was in the box..... i expected more than that. Stick to the sandbox mode and you'll have a blast especially with the DLC

The survival game portion of the game is great, but don't waste your time on the story mode. What a glitchy, scripted, steaming pile of nonsense. Play the survival mode and make up your own back story.

The game feels very repetitve, lots and lots of walking, stopping to light fires, then more walking, often at a snail's pace. 
The crafting system is very limited.
I spent about 40 hours playing, and it just isn't worth it.

I loved this game, it was my obsession when I started first playing it on PS4. When I got my computer I played through the whole story again because it was just that good of a game. After waiting years for the release of episode 5, it has left a deep deep cut in my heart. Everything the first 3 episodes were just thrown out the window, missed deadlines by a developing team who had put so much heart into the beginning episodes got my hopes up that we were waiting for the best episode of the series. Instead I got Wolves in tight spaces, a mine with stupid quips while I'm fighting to breath toxic gas from a lady scared of the dark... it was just so stupid, the writing was terrible, there was soooooooooooo much loot it wasn't survival, it was making sure I was empty enough to run. I must agree that the train, thou cool, was a huge waste of time. To many cutscenes, ever heard of show dont tell hinterland? And for Christ sake can we just shoot the main villain? Why is the trapper holding a gun just to shoot NPC nobody's. My 8 year old writes better stories, at least ones that conclude....I'm beyond disappointed with all of you hinterland, just just disappointed... ♥♥♥♥!

Episode 5 BAD, HORRIBLE, BORING. It's like a completely different game. To think poor fans of the series waited YEARS for chapter 5.... Appalling.
I immediatly uninstalled the game after playing. I just wish I could get my time spent on this game back. 
I got so enraged I got admitted into hospital and later went to the psych ward.

I can't understand how people with many hours in this game, will give a negative review just cause of the last episode...is episode 5 a bit of a let down ? Yes...the devs added too many (imo) side quests - or to be more accurate, too many narratives-  but then made it all streamlined,  I would've preferred  to maybe have  less of them, but ones where you let me roam and survive like in the first episodes.
HOWEVER...that is but a small dent in  how amazing this game is overall, and how many hours of amazing survival gameplay you can get from the challenges, free roam, and yes, even wintermute (the episodes)
Taking so long to generate such a mediocre completion to the wintermute story. Can not recommend this game to anyone interested in a good story mode.

Enjoyed Wintermute for a good while...

And then came the bear cave.
The absolute worst design of a survival game I have ever witnessed.

The stuff you collected for the last 12+ hours?
All gone. Enjoy.
The bear that somehow always magically knows where you are? 
Of course. Enjoy.
Sneaking that is slower than a sloth on morphine?
Totally. Makes sense. Enjoy.
YOU CANNOT SAVE.
YOU CANNOT SKIP THE MINUTE-LONG BEAR ATTACK CUTSCENES.
Each time it happens, all your gear just gets destroyed piece by piece.
Where you are supposed to go in that cave to find your weapon? 
100% unclear.
You are just supposed to suffer endlessly until you stumble upon it I guess. I chose the uninstall option instead.

Whoever designed this should be seriously ashamed of themselves and never touch game design again.
To ruin such a good experience with this absolute travesty is unforgivable.

"The climax forces Mackenzie out onto the fragile, frozen sea ice in the harbor for a final confrontation with Mathis. It's a brutal game of cat-and-mouse in the freezing dark. Remembering a lesson from Jeremiah, Mackenzie realizes he can't beat the convict in a test of pure strength. Instead, he uses the environment. He baits Mathis over a patch of thin ice weakened by the pulsing Aurora and shoots the ice at Mathis's feet. The ice gives way, and Mathis plunges into the freezing, dark depths of the ocean, ending his reign of terror for good."

Next time you make us stare at a disclaimer about how much you hate generative tools, make sure your human-written script isn't actively worse than a 5-second prompt.

the survival mode of this game is great

the storymode on the other hand..
it caught my interest to begin with but quickly fell through within itself. The characters are boring and i dont think they offer anything to help the player actually connect with them, if anything i get annoyed with the protagonists dialogue (very obvious that the writers are millennials)
its also obvious that they didnt plan far enough ahead in the games writing to consider the human npcs and their mechanics. jace looks dumb and wonky running around the player and i cant help but roll my eyes whenever she ''conveniently'' cant do anything to help the player. theyve been talking about the storymode for almost an entire decade and the episodes, especially episode 5, just dont live up to their word or the wait.
i am also not a fan of the obvious softlaunch for features that were just going to be added into episode 5 in the tales from the far territory dlc

its not a good game in terms of story and i definitely dont think an additional payment to access it is by any means worth it.
if you want a good, isolated, challenging survival game then the 20 usd option is totally worth it.

This game is special to me, It is the most pure survival game ever, and whilst some may call it borish or sluggish, To me it's anything but that.
The long dark's gameplay is hypnotizing, Addictive and Brutal, It's easy to sink 50 hours into this game within a short timespan because of how trancing it is, It's gameplay loop and variety create such an amazing combination of game-mechanics and it's assortment of areas in it's frost-cold open world create an amazing atmosphere.
This game isn't perfect, But it is simply a must-play for any fan of the survival genre.
Solid 8.5/10

There is no stamina for anything, you are always tired or dying over every little thing, you can't jump, you constantly get stuck in corners, you can't even run for 2 minutes. The game is kind of cool, but it is unplayable sometimes. Starting over from scratch every single time gets exhausting, it honestly becomes a drag to be completely honest, and this is with me only playing on Pilgrim mode, I can't imagine other levels! Not a chance. After dying hundreds of times for various reasons, I decided that playing this game is only good for messing around/fooling around, playing this seriously is just asking to feel angry all the time.
If you don't have patience for slowness and a scarcity of energy, I do not recommend this game.

-After spending a long time and a lot of patience to make the moose bag while trying to take this game seriously, I don't want to try anything else anymore, since it's not worth it. The only destiny that awaits you is dying and going back to zero every single time (no matter when or how)- 🚫

This game offers tremendous value for its low price. I've been playing it for the better part of a decade and have no regrets in playing the full experience. 

There will be some reviews of this game that specifically focus on Episode 5 - namely its long development time and underwhelming release. There are four other great chapters that lead there and built years of hype for Episode 5, all of which were worth playing. I just finished episode five and I have to agree that it is deeply flawed, but it is not the entire game.

Long Dark Offers a great survival simulator on its own with episodic story with great value for your investment. It is a worthy experience for your money and I would still recommend it despite how underwhelming episode five was.

Best game in the survival genre. I've played thousand+ hours in Survival mode on PS4 as well. Had to write a review as I'm seeing the recent mixed views are for part 5 of the story mode, which frankly most players don't play, and because they don't like the ending. Ridiculous. This is a sandbox survival mode which has only gotten better and better over time. The balanced survival mechanics, expansive world that feels like you are living in a water-colour painting and solitude will get its hooks into you early on and keep you coming back for more. The developers will be adding to it to improve Survival mode until they go full ham on Blackrock: The Long Dark 2.  If you like Stranded Deep, Subnautica, The Forest, etc. then you will love this one!
Really good game lots of story to it and it's a perfect game for those who want a mix of survival and story although some parts may be graphic at times. I highly recommend this game.

Don't play Wintermute; dont buy Wintermute. I think all of us early adopters can agree that it is at best a 5/10 telltale game that we waited 10+ years for. The beauty of this game is in survival mode. And it is so beautiful. The lonely landscapes, the pink-painted mountains that you know only your eyes have gazed upon, the ominous sound of a distant wolf howling, the sudden discovery of a tool that you prayed for days before. This game takes so much, but it also gives. You will die. But your death will always be preventable. You will learn from it. It will always be something that you could have avoided if you had planned a little better or just been a tad less reckless. That is the beauty of this game, that you are in control of your destiny, however long that may last. Nature doesn't want you dead, but it doesn't care if you live either. It will brew up terrible blizzards that leave you walking in circles until you freeze or fogs that leave you blind or predators that block the path you need to take. You are simply a visitor, sometimes an interloper, in this harsh world and it is up to you to last as long as you can. Much like life, it is up to you to write your own story.

Realistic Firewatch (disparingly)
While I may be giving this a thumbs up my review is mixed. I only played Wintermute (I have never been a sandbox guy) and man does it get very boring. Most of the fetch missions are basically move to very distant X place and retrieve object which will then point to Y place even further off. And the sidequests are basically pointless because any additional item you get you lose them for some reason.
The game is very detailed: you face wolves, bears, cougars, moose, fight off hunger, cold, fatigue and thirst, heal yourself of injuries, sprains, even dysentery. It looks very good also, the voice acting is great, and the faint bits of music add to the atmosphere. 
BUT WHAT IS IT WITH WALKING SIMULATORS AND NOT BEING ABLE TO JUMP?? And why did they add all those puzzles? The bear crawl, the steam pipes, even the train tracks. And for some reason almost every episode ends with some kind of maze in the dark no less and it just feels like padding gametime.
As for the story without any spoilers I believe it could've been done better (after all they had a decade to work this out) and there are some plotholes and a massive exposition dump that takes away from it all. As a survival game I am sure it is great but as a story it becomes very dull very fast.
I called it Realistic Firewatch because that too had endless walking and a dud of an ending and all I have to say at the end is thank God for auto-walk.

Canadian winter simulator. 

A very good survivor game really which I played on other platforms before and got it on Steam as well for 2-3 € on sale.

Probably the most immersive and overall best survival game I've ever played. However, it is a very slow-burn kind of a game and often you'll have to set goals and challenges for yourself (Unless you're playing Wintermute, the story mode) because there aren't that many objectives the game gives you in the survival mode (There are a few added by the "Tales From The Far Territory" DLC).

The game has challenge modes too, which have been fun but require a lot of patience. I'd love to see more challenges added though because there's a lot of potential for challenges.

As far as I understand, this game's development is slowly approaching it's end and they'll probably shift onto Blackfrost or some other project. Before that happens, I'd love it if the devs integrated a mod manager to the game itself and gave the community the tools to make their own challenges, because there's a lot of cool potential for challenges that the devs just can't fully realize by themselves AND it would be a way for the community to keep the game fresh for as long as there is one for the game.

Love this game, still going to rate it favorable for everything it does right (except episode 5 of wintermute)... but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ the cable car scene was awful. Like, I think it took a huge amount of effort to make it that bad. Like, could it move slower than that for no reason at all? Could Jace not have jumped in the car while you stayed on the other side? Does she not have hands to help you spin the lever? It's absolutely infuriating.
Edit: I really didn't think it could get worse. And it did, like unimaginable levels of "I actually didn't think this could be this bad" Also, stupid henchman, doesn't he know if a flare touches your toe, you get instantly pulled towards the center of the earth?
Sorry guys, but this is just pathetic, but like whatever, we're moving on to TLD2 so, whatever whatever. Just don't waste your time with episode 5.

I don't really know what it is about this game that makes it so good. It's hard. It can be quite unforgiving. But it's also beautiful. It is truly one of those games where you can lose yourself for hours on end. Exploration in sandbox mode is top notch, and the terror is one of silence, loneliness, and despair rather than zombies and monsters. Walking through the snow at night with wolves on your trail and a wound that is slowly ebbing your life away, then actually making it to your shelter and surviving is hugely rewarding. I love it.
The best survival game ever.  I have been living a second life in this world since it was launched.  Beautiful, meloncholy and gripping. 5 out of 5.

Honestly, after finishing wintermute all i can say is im disappointed. I won't speak to survival as, I haven't really played it all that much. Played on Hardened Survivor. 

Like some of the other reviewers ive supported this game for a while. Bought the survival expansions even though i had no interest, just because it felt like the story side of things, even though clunky at times, was an interesting spin to try to make a story game with brutal survival mechanics. Chapters 1-3 are honestly kinda good; the story is intriguing, mysterious, and really just lends to the already campy feeling the games got with its aesthetic. The story and this aesthetic really falls apart, however, with chapters 4-5, as it seems as if the devs realized they bit off a bit more than they could chew, and couldn't figure out a satisfying way to end their story. Instead of trying to make the game end in a satisfying way, hinterland decided to announce a sequel game that would answer all the loose end of the first game. Unfortunately this meant that chapters 4 and 5 were sort of pointlessly wishy washy, answering some questions the game poised in the first chapter, but in most cases making more problems just for the sake of extending their already barebones story for a second game. Chapter 5 is especially bad with this, I appreciate the work that seemed to have gone into making the cutscenes, but most of them just feel like a waste of time, and drama manufactured for the sake of a second game. I ended up finishing the game due to pure spite, and continued to feel frustration at every turn the story took in chapter 5 due to how much it seemed as if the plot point decision was to make a second game. 

If you're interested in a semi-realistic (still pretty arcade though) survival game, the pure survival side of things seem fine. However, if you're buying TLD for wintermute, be prepared for frustration beyond end, a pointless conclusion, and plot holes galore.

Unskippable, intros, dialogue & Cutscenes:
On the subject of unskippable dialogue & Cust scenes, this game was infested with them to the point it became grating, then irksome, then so annoying you begin to hate the game. As I bought the complete game - I DID NOT NEED TO BE REMINDED OF THE PREVIOUS EPISODE... or to have to listen to the bloody title song 5x times... @Hinterland - once the player has seen the UNSKIIPPABLE intro's 1-2 times, they DO NOT NEED TO KEEP seeing them or your logo, preventing players from disabling them is just myopic, misguided and anti-consumer.

Then there are the unskippable dialogue scenes, I was able to skip through most of Jeramiah's ramblings, but had to sit through Molly's monotonous manipulations and EVERY BLOODY NPC after that point.... WHY? I could see what they were going to whittle on about from a mile away.... But instead I had to sit through filler, pauses, and animations that again I could not have cared less about.

What made ALL of the above aggravating was that the pacing was off a lot of the time, so having the ability to skip through filter, pointless pauses and ear-bending would have greatly alleviated this pain point.

Constantly getting stuck on geometry:
Getting stuck on a tiny shard of wood or something else poking out of the snow for the hundredth time, got real tedious real fast. There is absolutely no bloody need for this. I design & create game levels myself, so @Hinterland you have no bloody excuse, its just lazy level design that only serves to annoy players.

No jump mechanic:
I have zero ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ patience for games that remove the ability to do this. It is again, just lazy level design.

TLD Community hub:
I tried to vent my frustrations with one of the characters in this game, but the Hinterland mods seem unable to handle a bit course language. So I wouldn't waste your time there, if you get stuck like I did a number of times, you would be better served by one of many guides and videos online instead. 

Pointless Ending: 
Er how the flying ♥♥♥♥ did Makenzie implausibly loose all the clothing, weapons and gear he got from the bunker. Stupid, moronic ♥♥♥♥ like this in games is beyond annoying. This was the most anticlimactic - not allowing the player to kill that POS Mathis was a mistake, that ruined the ending. When Makenzie told the others to take Astrid with them, I thought finally, time to put that rabid dog down, but no. Instead what we got a was several more unskippable cutscenes, with filler and delays I honestly gave a ♥♥♥♥ about

Lastly I got this game on sale for dirt cheap, so it was something to play.

I enjoy this game a lot. I enjoy games with quiet apocalypses where the only thing you have to worry about is the world itself. The silence of the world as I'm making a long journey across the map and the howling of a blizzard that caught me off-guard in one my playthroughs will always haunt me. The lack of people (save for the Trader from the Tales DLC, but does he really count?) makes me feel alone in a way that is both comforting and soul crushing all at once. You are alone in a world that does not care for you.

If you like a survival game where your biggest enemy is battling the cold and the steady decline of your own wellbeing while contending with wildlife, then you will enjoy this game. There are varying difficulties and even a sandbox mode where you can customize many aspects of the game. I recommend Tales from the Far Territory. It is a great DLC for the survival mode and brings many more things to enhance or spice up your playthroughs.

Just stay away from the WINTERMUTE story DLC or whatever they separated it into. It is a badly written, contrived, and disappointing piece of media. The characters themselves are solid, but their execution is lackluster and the main characters makes so many stupid decisions that it's obvious the writer doesn't know how to get him from point A to point B without sacrificing the player's agency or standard common sense. The ending was more disappointing than the Red vs Blue finale, which at the time was the most disappointed I have been with a piece of media in a long, long time. I am tired of streamers and YouTubers refusing to give the story any criticism simply because they are "friends" of the devs or got early access. The story had a solid start, but the narrative nosedives into contradictions and character decisions that are completely absurd all for the sake of getting to the ending. The "bittersweet" ending is not earned and was insulting to watch. It felt like someone spat in my face. Watch someone play it if you really want the story. Do not waste your money. Don't listen to the main streamers or YouTubers. WINTERMUTE is a pile of garbage with a few tiny, miniscule gems caked in grease and somebody's moldy leftovers.

I hold no resentment for the people of Hinterland, and I think they should all take pride in the fact that they brought The Long Dark to life. Making a game is a huge achievement. However, even though my interest lies strictly in the survival mode, I will not be following Hinterland or their upcoming releases. I was excited for Blackfrost when it was announced, but I am not going to be waiting another 10 years for a game that I fear will ultimately be a bigger disappointment. I have since removed it from my wishlist, and I have no interest in following its development. Good luck to you all, to the devs and to those willing to support them, but this is where I get off. I don't even really want to play this game anymore. I hope the devs learn from their mistakes and create something better in the future, and I hope that the people who stick with them don't feel this level of disappointment from their future projects.

To any of my friends who were curious and read this: this is just the polite version of the 3 day long crashout I had when Episode 5 released. Thanks for sticking with me while I lost my mind lol

I was surprised to like the game so much. The Wintermute storyline is very interesting and the characters were not one dimensional. I suppose women were very present in the game studio because not one dialogue made me uncomfortable. The anti-AI statement was the cherry on the top. I very much recommend it as a survival gamer myself.

Though the end of Wintermute was a bit rough and very bumpy, I have a deep seated love for the survival mode and all the experiences it alone has given me. If you want a good, cold, and quiet apocalypse, I recommend this game a ton along with the Tales From The Far Territory dlc to spice up any normal survival run, great stuff.

Story mode is utterly disappointing:

- Unskippable cutscenes during the first playthrough (only in chapters 3, 4, and 5, which happen to be the longest).
- Poorly written dialogue, and it really didn't feel immersive for me due to some of the ridiculous ideas presented, such as the use of magical objects to defeat certain opponents (not fitting for a survival game in my opinion).
- Not particularly challenging in comparison to the game's survival mode.

It pains me to leave a negative review, because the survival mode is great, but i feel like the story mode is so poorly executed that it makes the whole game feel underwhelming.

Disclaimer: this review is in regards to Wintermute only.

I bought this game around ten years ago, played it once, got annoyed by weight/stamina system and never returned to it. I didn't even finish episode 1. That being said, I did see promise in the story. I thought maybe waiting for the story to be done would take... maybe 2 years? 4 years? It was not high on my list of games to try just with the first impression I was given, so the waiting was negligible for me, but for others it was an extremely long wait. 

Recently, I got a steam library notification saying episode 5 is out, the game is complete! I thought "what better time to give it another shot now that 10 years has passed?". I started a playthrough on the easiest difficulty.

I do episode 1, it felt familiar despite last playing it all those years ago. It was fine, there were a few mechanics that weren't well explained but maybe I missed something.

I do episode 2 and 3, it felt like none of my progress was contributing towards anything. Every time i returned back from a quest, the absolute CRUMB of story I would get was not motivating in the slightest. It felt like chores the game. Go here, do chores, get zero rewards (even in the form of storytelling) and when the chapter ends.... cliffhanger. It was at this point I feel like the game should have been called "the long walk". Some other reviewers really like the slow, methodical survival gameplay - albeit most are talking about the survival mode itself. The story mode is incredibly a time wasting simulator. If you are someone like me who does not like looking at walkthroughs unless you absolutely have to, this is going to be painful. Not that the puzzles / quests are that difficult - but moreso that anytime you want to backtrack to a place where you might have missed something it absolutely KILLS time. You can call that realistic, high stakes survival gaming - I call it a waste of time. I'm not expecting to be teleported to every objective, but I wasn't expecting to have my character huff and puff in my ear while moving at the pace of a snail like a diabetic turtle with glaucoma - only to have to turn around again because i missed some obscure note or key in a building I was in on the other side of the map.

Episode 4 begins, the story seems like its going somewhere. I was actually liking the way this chapter was feeding you the story. I finally felt like my quests were leading to at least some reveal of plot. I made it to the mines, spent quite a bit of time completing that section because there is one specific valve arm you can remove - while the other ones all stick and are unremovable. Maybe I missed something, but i shrug it off because I will get more story reveal when i make it back to the "main area" of that chapter. I die on the way back due to my own incompetence. I thought "haha woops! silly me, I've done a lot though so reloading the last checkpoint should be fine" I was set back 45 real life minutes worth of progress. I am not going to bother finishing the story due to how much time it makes you waste.

People say the survival mode is great. I really liked the art direction and the disclaimer at the beginning of the game I felt like had the right spirit - but this story mode is not fun or engaging in the slightest. Given other reviews I don't feel like I'm missing much with the "great reveal" at the end.

In terms of the number of delays for Wintermute (episode five), the history goes like this:
First series of delays (2022–2023): Originally, the release was expected much earlier after episode four (2021), but development was pushed back due to the game being split into Survival and Story modes and DLC development.
2023 delay: Hinterland originally targeted the end of 2023, but in December they announced a delay to 2024.
2024 delay: In November 2024, another announcement came that the episode would miss the end of the year and would be released in January 2025.
The latest delay to 2026: The January deadline was not met either, and the final date was set for March 31, 2026, when the episode was finally released.
In total, the fifth episode of Wintermute has been delayed at least four times compared to the specific announced dates or expected time windows.

TLDR - It's a pretty good survival game and well worth picking up if you're into the genre. But the survival stuff is probably a bit too hard core if you're not and I'm not sure the story mode is worth it by itself. You'd be better off starting with Subnautica, The Forest or something similar. 

This is one of those games where story mode and survival mode are separate things - think Green Hell rather than The Forest or Subnautica. I can see why they did that but it does have downsides. 

Story mode felt very easy - the survival stuff and death was really toned down. That's fine - it's story mode - but I thought the plot didn't really carry it, it was fairly bare bones and the gameplay rapidly turned into what felt like fetch quests. I got maybe half way through episode 2 before I got bored and switched. 

Survival mode is really where the game shines. There's lots of stuff to learn, lots of stuff to do, lots of stuff to craft. Unlike a lot of the genre where, once you're set up, the challenge is gone, here you're always just one bear, wolf pack or blizzard away from a short, sharp end.  The game does a good job of keeping the challenge up.

But, because it's totally separate from story mode, survival mode can feel a little directionless once you've got a basic set up going. There's no overarching objective beyond surviving the next day and, precisely because the world is so dangerous, I can't see why a rational person would leave their nice safe shelter to strike out into the hostile unknown. At least on lower difficulties, the game doesn't really give you a good reason to explore. Once you've hit a couple of zones, you're probably set for tools and once you've done one forge run, there's not much reason to go back.

Indeed, even when I did move zone, I usually found found that, most places, 95% of the loot was in two or three main locations, usually along a road or railway as you'd expect, and the risk-reward ratio for exploring the rest of the zone just wasn't really there. You might find a cabin or something but it's probably going to have a mouldy chocolate bar in it. Or a rusty tin of sardines. Or a thin wool sweater riddled with holes. But you probably won't and will get stuck in a blizzard and die instead. It's certainly more realistic but put me off exploring - I learned rapidly that striking out to see what was over there was a recipe for trouble and almost never worth it.

The problem with that is that, if you stay put, the survival stuff can start feeling a bit like work. While the initial challenge of acquiring food, fuel, water etc in a new zone is engaging, the actual mechanics of doing it aren't that much fun in themselves day to day.  Once you're set up, you're going to find yourself spending a lot of time chopping firewood, cooking meat, boiling water, repairing clothes. Even stuff like hunting could feel like hard work. The bow felt pretty hit and miss to me and, if you just wound them, animals run *forever.* It's tedious tracking them down when you walk slowly at the best of times, walk glacially if there's even a mild breeze blowing your way, and sprain your ankle on even the mildest slopes. 

So, if you do stay put, you're probably going to get bored. Chopping firewood, cooking food and boiling pots of water isn't *that* much fun. Even if it makes no rational sense to leave, you really do need to move on to keep the game interesting. And there is stuff out there that's worth grabbing, even if I suspect most people would die long before they found it by themselves. I ended up googling a bit to give myself something to aim for and which direction to head. And, to be fair, I ended up having a great adventure going through Ash canyon and then eventually summiting Timberwolf mountain. I'm just not sure I'd have gone there if I hadn't known there was stuff  that was worth getting at the end. Hell, I probably wouldn't have bothered climbing the first rope. Because most of the time there really is just a mouldy chocolate bar, a rusty tin of sardines, or a sweater riddled with holes.

To be fair, there's 5 different difficulties and a lot of people just play it just to see how long they can survive on each.  For me though, I thought the game would have benefited from at least some kind of end goal -  look for other survivors, call for help, get off the island etc - to drive you forwards, to "go deeper" and give you a reason to leave your base and risk new zones. But, like I say, the world's interesting, the survival stuff is well done, and the challenge level keeps it interesting for a good long time. While, after a hundred or so hours, I felt I'd boiled enough pots of water and wanted a change, I could see myself coming back for another run on a higher difficulty down the line.

Tech stuff - It's an indie game - you're not going to get AAA graphics - but I thought the aesthetic was spot on, the world was beautiful to wander though and the zones were well put together. Plus it's very easy on the graphics card and super stable. I don't think I encountered any crashes or significant bugs. The UI is a bit clunky but, for the most part, stuff was reasonably intuitive. I didn't find myself googling "how the ♥♥♥♥ do you do x?" every five minutes anyway.

The game is great but not perfect.

The Long Dark is one of those games that defines a genre: in the vast market of open-end survival games, it stands out in many ways, spurring a number of clear 2nd generation derivations for those looking for the experience of role-playing your way through harsh frozen terrains. You get an occasional negative review here on Steam – some of which I think is just a backlash for elevated expectations and some which express genuine issues – so the game is not perfect. I will try to cover both the good and the bad.

The good
* Pacing – the game is slow by design. Sure, you can speed-run your way through the highlights of Great Bear Island and grab all the best gear in a matter few hours of playtime, but in its most best form, patience is the key. You will need to coop up for days, waiting for the weather to settle, crafting and resource preparation such as leather curing requires up to 2 in-game weeks with no way to shortcut it. Those that rush and venture out ill-prepared may find themselves in a sudden and irreversible demise. From a more technical design choice, pacing is also directed by completely removing jumping – otherwise a typical staple of any first- or third-person game.

* Environment mechanics – the main adversary in the game is not whatever arbitrary monster / enemy, not even the wildlife looking for an easy lunch. It is the environment around you. A change in the weather or a misplaced step on an attempt to shortcut over a hill, can make a leisurely afternoon walk into a struggle for life and death. The Long Dark incorporates several cool features (most notably wind and ambient temperature mechanics) to make environmental challenges far more than just stuffing your character with enough of the good stuff to counter whatever debuffs. If you’re stuck in a blizzard and lose your bearings, your options for survival can narrow down very fast.

* Static world – TLD has no destructible environment, limited base building or physics-based interactions. This is essentially a technical limitation, but it works well with the overall handicraft feel of the game. I am reminded of Morrowind above all in it that each location is feels being carefully created by hand. With enough time, the player will come to learn each bend of the road, frozen lake or fallen tree across a ravine. There is real beauty in this static frozen landscape which I imagine has been a painstaking effort of level design.

* Less is more – this is another limitation of TLD that works for its favor. New content is slow to come at times, but each item has a specific purpose and its own distinct feel (in particularly weaponry). This is a stark contrast to having many variations of the same content with different stats and texture.

The okay enough
* Wildlife AI – in contrast to environment mechanics, wildlife AI is pretty mediocre. There are some interesting dynamics (such as wolves hunting prey) but in general, the wildlife is there for the player to hunt or fight against with little variation of behavior. The way the game re-spawns wildlife when changing the scene can be frustrating at times – suppose you used up your last good bullet to take out a bear and decide to wait it out in a nearby cabin, the game can under certain conditions re-spawn you a new one in full health just across the street.

* Click-based minigames – on occasion, the game lets you speed-click your left mouse button to brawl with a wolf or  (in story-mode) accomplish some arbitrary task. I understand this is to convey a sense of urgency for the player, but this eventually becomes tedious as there is no way to reasonably avoid these interactions.
 
The bad
* Bugs that have become features – there are several mechanical quirks that should be considered bugs, but have been around for so long that they have become staple gameplay elements. The scent system is one such: you are a homing beacon for every predator in 10 miles if you’re carrying a piece of cooked meat. However, when you drop it from the inventory in front of you, the effect is negated. Pick it up again and drop it again as a decoy and kaboom – that piece of meat is back to being a homing beacon. Synchronization with Steam Cloud is also problematic and can reset hours of gameplay - for a game that does not allow manual saving, this is very bad. Best to turn off Steam Cloud synchronization and hope it never gets toggled on again.

* Wintermute (story mode) – more on this below, but there are some systematic problems with the story mode, so for best experience, do the survival mode ad nauseum before turning to Wintermute.

A few words about the different play modes / DLC-s

* Survival mode – this is the part and parcel of TLD. Everything written above essentially applies to survival mode. Go an sink a few hundred hours to it.

* Tales from the Far Territory DLC – contrary to some negative reviews, this DLC is pretty good. It builds well on-top of the core game, adding new content, new mechanics and new unique challenges. The new maps that come as part of this DLC are challenging, varied and distinctly different from the core game. The tales / narrative events mechanic actually works rather well with survival mode, adding newfound sense of direction.

* Wintermute DLC (the story mode) – this is the problem child of the whole TLD franchise. On paper, the idea is great, adding quintessential background lore for the quiet apocalypse, but the implementation has some serious flaws.

Essentially Wintermute introduces a watered down version of the core game: the difficulty is tuned all the way down (which would be fine in its own) and the open-world concept channeled into awkwardly executed linear story-telling. The episodic nature of Wintermute means different parts of the DLC were released in a period of over 9 years, but unlike the core game, earlier episodes do not seem to be up to date with the years of development and QoL improvements. Withholding some content updates I can understand (e.g, having a mountain lion rip you apart in Episode 1 would make Mackenzie’s later ordeals feel a tad anticlimactic), but this also seems to include some essential technical optimizations. For that reason, Episodes 1 & 2 run like rough demos of the latter more polished version of the game. Episodes 3 & 4 are perhaps Wintermute at its best – a more condensed version of the core game with more stuff to do on a single region of the game. Episode 5 takes a whole new turn and consists of a number of non-connected segments each a mini-game in its own right.

Wintermute introduces a number of NPCs, a welcome change from the desolate solitude of the core game. Unfortunately, these NPCs are little more than pre-scripted dialog boxes and have no impact to the actual gameplay. I suppose this reflects the difficulty of integrating more complex NPC interaction to the existing open world setup. 

At times, Wintermute is so cut-scene heavy it runs more like a visual novel than a game. So the obvious question: what’s the story like? I’d say, much like the DLC itself, a mixed bag. On one hand, the story tries to strike a more serious tone than your typical zombie apocalypse slasher (looking at you, Dying Light), but falls short on the narrative consistency and character development. We are given the outline of the story, characters and the emotions they are supposed to feel, but it comes off as just a bit too stretched. With full voice-acting and complex animated cut scenes, Wintermute has the form and ambition of a AAA studio held back by the budgetary constraints a small indie company.

So there’s that. If you read it this far, you’ll probably want to buy the game all the same. At the time of writing this, TLD sequel has been announced for over a year, so there’s a good chance that by the time the Great Bear Island has exhausted its nooks and corners, you can already turn to the next game on the series.

Игра дает уникальный опыт, что наверно самое важное в играх. Это инди игра, с отличными механиками выживания, с классной атмосферой, погружением в природу с красивыми видами.

Последний эпиздод (5-ый) сильно расстроил, что на вопрос "Порекомендуете ли игру своим друзьям" думал сказать Нет. Но в совокупности все же игра интересная. 
Видимо на 5-ый эпизод у разработчиков совсем не было времени и денег. Сделано максимально в стиле тяп-ляп. Куча кат-сцен, роликов, которые двигают сюжет, рассказывают о мире, вместо того, чтобы ты сам мог изучать окружение и получать это порционно проявляя свое любопытство. Еще добавили механик противоречащих настроению предыдущих эпизодов, где тебе нужно спешить, ломиться сквозь врагов, или идти в скрытом режиме через бандитов, когда ты ничего не можешь толком сделать. Разложили повсюду кучу ресурсов (одежды, оружие, еды), которую нет смысла собирать - все проходится в касание. А я собирал, таскал все с собой, ждал, когда пригодится. В итоге при смене главных героев у меня еще и отнимали нажитое... В общем выживания и погружения в мир в 5-ом эпизоде почти отсутствовал. Ну и куча багов, недоделок только расстраивали. Спасибо, что пытались, но мне кажется лучше бы выпустить было комикс/видеоролик с завершением истории вместо такой игры, если хотели поставить точку.

This game kind of pulls me in both directions when it comes to my opinion of it. 

The survival mode is one of the better experiences out there for just trying to live while in a harsh environment. IMO, It is best played on the middle difficulty because the survival bars are just punishing enough and the scarcity of supplies does come into affect, but your not typically desperate at every moment. With good planning you can survive for a long time, but if you get lax on your hunting and fishing, you can suddenly find yourself struggling. The game also has some very good exploration to it, and it was the main thing that kept me going. Finding a new point of interest, or stumbling upon a new building always had that sense of wonder for me. You never really know what your going to find in some of those places, and if there is going to be that one tool that you haven't found yet that will make your life easier. 

Unfortunately, I started playing the game in the story mode, so when I actually started the survival mode I had seen a good bit of the map and it took away some of that enjoyment. The story mode really isn't doing anything for me, and IMO ends in a bad way. They explored some interesting concepts in there, but I just don't like how it was all wrapped up.

I do have some complaints. First off, they really gameified the predators in the game, with wolfs in particular  being just an absolute annoyance. If they are anywhere near you they will start following you, which is only a real issue if they are directly in the direction you need to head. If they start following from anywhere else, they walk at your walking speed, so they are no threat. They even mention in the start-up screens that wolves don't act this way. Why do this? They are just so annoying to deal with, and the game can already be hard enough. They even created a mechanic in the game to make it so that it was a negative to kill and then eat the wolves, because they kept throwing so many wolves at people and they had meat on them. They should have made the wolves more competitive enemies. Constantly killing the prey in the area, so that if you don't deal with the wolves, finding deer and rabbits is harder until you kill the wolves off. 

Other things I was not a fan of in the game is just the size of the maps, and therefore, the time it takes to get anywhere in the game. Running in this game is incredibly inefficient and will drain your energy bar so fast, meaning you can't do certain actions once you get to where you are going. This pushes you to just walk everywhere, and the maps are SO LARGE that it takes so much time to travel anywhere. Add onto that a wind system that slows you to a crawl even if you are not carrying a large weight and for some reason always seemed to be blowing from the direction I was trying to travel, and you get a game that supposed to be about survival that turns into mostly just walking. I was so bored during so much of this game. They gameified the wolves to be unending enemies that will rarely stop pursuing you, but they don't gameify the traveling? Why would my character, who by the time I quit my survival play-through had been in that environment for close to half a year, not have become stronger and healthier during that time? No mechanic to make sprinting more energy efficient was a total mistake, and really kept me from fully exploring the map, and DEFINITELY from buying the DLC.

The Long Dark is a masterpiece of the survival genre. It strips away the distractions of modern games and forces you to face the most dangerous enemy of all: Mother Nature herself. It is quiet, brutal, and hauntingly beautiful.

Why I highly recommend it:

Unmatched Atmosphere: There is no other game that captures the feeling of solitude like this. The howling wind, the crunching snow under your boots, and the eerie silence of the Canadian wilderness create an atmosphere that stays with you long after you stop playing.

True Survival Challenge: This isn't a game about shooting zombies; it's about managing your calories, fighting off hypothermia, and deciding whether to risk a blizzard for a chance at finding extra fuel. Every decision feels heavy and meaningful.

Stunning Art Style: The stylized, painterly visuals are breathtaking. Whether it’s a calm sunset over a frozen lake or a deadly storm rolling over the mountains, every moment looks like a piece of art.

The "Long Dark" Feeling: The game forces you to slow down and respect your environment. It teaches you that sometimes, the best way to survive is to know when to seek shelter and wait out the storm.

If you are looking for a game that tests your patience, strategy, and resilience, this is it. It is a slow-burn experience that is as rewarding as it is challenging. Stay warm out there.

Verdict: 10/10 – The definitive survival experience.

I have been playing this game off and on over the years, clocking in a ton of hours in survival mode in between releases of Wintermute (story mode). I'll talk about them separately to give as best of an overview as I can of what to expect.

STORY MODE: Wintermute takes you through 5 episodes of varying gameplay from the perspectives of two characters with a complicated, sad backstory (this will be completely spoiler-free). Episodes 1-3 are spectacular at introducing these characters and orienting the player to its survival elements, which Hinterland has fine-tuned over time. Episode 4 feels more like an action/escape film, and episode 5 relies on some painful gameplay to punctuate its sentence. The overall story is well worth investing in, and while episode 5 relies heavily on exposition, it tells you what you need to know and sets you up nicely for a sequel. I do think more discovery of the exposition elements would have improved episode 5, but after ten years, I'm fairly satisfied with how the story has been told so far.

SURVIVAL MODE: Quite simply, it is some of the most satisfying gameplay you will find, The pace is slow, and relies completely on you fighting against the elements. There are difficulty levels to vary your experience, and so it is incredibly difficult to get bored once you have acclimated to its pace. I've had long runs in survival mode that really test your ability to keep going when you run out of supplies like prepackaged food and ammo. It forces you to scavenge, create, and build. I had a moose crush my ribs, but then I killed it and ate it for 12 days while burning through my medical supplies to heal. That's survival mode. 

Overall, it's been a spectacular way to spend far too many hours of my time. I'm confident I'll revisit it while waiting for its sequel, Blackfrost, to come out.

The sandbox survival part of the game alone is worth the price, I could easily sink a hundred more hours into just exploring each map and trying to not be mauled along the way. Just be warned the gameplay is purposefully slow in comparison to some other titles of the genre.

The optional storymode aspect has some ups and downs in terms of enjoyment, but I believe it serves as a really great jumping point for teaching the game mechanics, the world and its lore (great btw!), as well as pushing the player through challenges they normally would pass in favor of survival. 

And since everyone is discussing ep 5, I'll add my thoughts for the devs. It was fun! The cargo ship section alone made the whole experience of playing worth it, I loved every second of part 1. The gameplay/environment for part 2 were also enjoyable, but the writing for certain events flip-flopped between silly and frustrating to watch. The second-half of it was great for the art and the doom-lite experience of mowing down super wolves with a shotgun—my only sadness comes from not being allowed to keep it into the finale! Part 3 had a functioning train-bulldozer so 9/10, one point deducted for not being able to squish the pesky wolves that lollygagged on the tracks lol. And the ending, being pure story, I have...mixed feelings for; I would say the greatest weakness of it was Mathis himself. He was a villain I felt the story bent over backwards to keep alive and relevant rather than his character dominating IN SPITE of the harsh world and protagonists. The fates of Mack and Astrid I felt were also fitting, albeit it is despite of the execution (again, mostly because of Mathis' involvement). Overall, it's a good game and I'm look forward to what will be done with Blackfrost!
